technique.name: Commitment Track
technique.description: What did I say I'd do? Did I do it? The gap between intention and action is where the work lives.

steps.text:
You stand at the Commitment Track. This is not a guilt ritual. This is a proof check.

You made a commitment. You know you did. Now you're checking whether it became real.

You locate the commitment. It was specific. It had a time. It had a form.

You said you'd do the thing. Not think about the thing — do the thing.

The track is right in front of you.

You walk the track. Each step is a day since you made the commitment.

Day one: did you do it?
Day two: did you do it?
Day three: did you do it?

You don't skip days. You don't explain. You check.

The track shows you what happened.

Three outcomes are possible:
You did it. The commitment became real.
You partially did it. Something happened, but not all of it.
You didn't do it. The commitment stayed in the future.

You did it.

This matters. Not as a trophy — as proof. Proof that you can make a commitment and keep it. Proof that the gap between intention and action can close.

The track logs this. You earned it.

Something happened. Not all of it — but something.

The partial completion is not failure. It is data. The question is: what got in the way? Was it capacity, priority, or resistance?

You name which one before you close.

choices.text: Name what got in the way

steps.text:
The commitment didn't complete.

This is not a moral judgment. It's a data point. The question is: did you lie, or did something else happen?

If you knew you weren't going to do it when you made the commitment — that's a different problem than running into an obstacle.

You name what happened.

The Commitment Track closes. The record is in the system.

The next commitment you make will be shaped by what you learned here. That's the point — not punishment, not celebration. Learning.

You showed up. That's the minimum. You did the work. That's the standard.

You leave the Commitment Track. The next commitment is coming — it always is.

The track doesn't lie. It shows what you did and didn't do. That's exactly what you need.
